questions.question_text: Arizona is one of how many community property states?
distractors: Five
Twelve
Seven
questions.explanation: Arizona is one of 9 community property states (along with CA, ID, LA, NV, NM, TX, WA, and WI). Property acquired during marriage is presumed to be community property.

questions.question_text: A married couple in Arizona buys a home during their marriage using joint earnings. The deed lists only the husband's name. The home is presumed to be:
distractors: The husband's sole and separate property
Tenancy in common between the spouses
Joint tenancy with right of survivorship
questions.explanation: In Arizona, property acquired during marriage using community funds is presumed community property, regardless of how the deed is titled. The deed listing only one spouse's name does not change this presumption.

questions.question_text: A wife owned a condo before marriage. After the wedding, is this property community or separate?
questions.correct_answer: Sole and separate property of the wife
distractors: Community property after the marriage
Joint tenancy with the husband
Community property with right of survivorship
questions.explanation: Property owned before marriage remains the separate property of that spouse. Community property only applies to property acquired during the marriage. The condo stays the wife's sole and separate property.

questions.question_text: A married couple wants to sell their community property home in Arizona. Both spouses must:
questions.correct_answer: Sign the listing agreement and deed
distractors: Only the spouse on the deed must sign
Either spouse can sign independently
Only the spouse with the higher income
questions.explanation: Both spouses must join in any disposition or encumbrance of community real property (ARS 25-214(C)), so both sign the deed — and in practice both sign the listing agreement to bind the community. Neither spouse can unilaterally sell or encumber community real estate.

questions.question_text: A husband inherits a vacation home during the marriage. This property is classified as:
distractors: Community property of both spouses
Joint tenancy between the spouses
Community property with right of survivorship
questions.explanation: Property received by gift or inheritance during marriage is the separate property of the receiving spouse, even though it was acquired during the marriage. Only property purchased with community funds is community property.

questions.question_text: What is the key feature of joint tenancy that distinguishes it from tenancy in common?
distractors: Unequal ownership shares
Required spousal relationship
Requirement of equal possession
questions.explanation: Joint tenancy includes the right of survivorship — when one owner dies, their share automatically passes to the surviving joint tenant(s). Tenancy in common has no survivorship right; the deceased's share goes through their estate.

questions.question_text: Two unmarried friends buy a house together in Arizona without specifying the form of ownership. They hold title as:
distractors: Joint tenants with survivorship
Community property owners
Tenants by the entirety
questions.explanation: Tenancy in common is the default form of co-ownership for unmarried persons in Arizona when no other form is specified. Joint tenancy requires express language creating survivorship rights.

questions.question_text: Arizona allows married couples to hold title as "community property with right of survivorship." What is the main advantage of this form?
questions.correct_answer: It avoids probate on the first spouse's death
distractors: It provides stronger creditor protection
It allows unequal ownership shares
It eliminates the need for title insurance
questions.explanation: Community property with right of survivorship combines community property benefits with automatic transfer to the surviving spouse, avoiding probate. The property passes directly without court proceedings.

questions.question_text: Three friends own a property as joint tenants. One friend sells their interest to a stranger. The stranger and the remaining two friends now hold title as:
questions.correct_answer: TIC with the stranger; joint tenants between the two
distractors: All three as joint tenants with survivorship
All three as tenants in common equally
The sale is void without the others' consent
questions.explanation: When a joint tenant sells their interest, it breaks the joint tenancy as to that share. The buyer becomes a tenant in common with the remaining joint tenants, who still hold joint tenancy between themselves.

questions.question_text: Does Arizona recognize tenancy by the entirety?
questions.correct_answer: No, Arizona does not recognize this form of ownership
distractors: Yes, for all married couples
Yes, but only for homestead properties
Only if both spouses are on the deed
questions.explanation: Arizona does not recognize tenancy by the entirety. Married couples can hold title as community property, community property with right of survivorship, joint tenancy, or tenancy in common.

questions.question_text: What is a beneficiary deed in Arizona?
questions.correct_answer: A deed transferring property on death to a beneficiary
distractors: A deed given to the lender as loan security
A deed transferring property into a living trust
A deed issued by the court after full probate
questions.explanation: A beneficiary deed is authorized by ARS 33-405 — it allows property to transfer on the owner's death to a named beneficiary without going through probate. It must be recorded during the owner's lifetime and is revocable.

questions.question_text: A homeowner records a beneficiary deed naming their daughter as beneficiary. Can the homeowner later change their mind?
distractors: No, once recorded it is irrevocable
Only with the beneficiary's written consent
Only by court order
questions.explanation: Beneficiary deeds are revocable during the owner's lifetime. The owner can record a new beneficiary deed naming a different person or revoke the deed entirely without the beneficiary's consent.

questions.question_text: For a beneficiary deed to be effective in Arizona, it must be:
distractors: Notarized and filed after the owner's death
Approved by the probate court first
Signed by the named beneficiary
questions.explanation: A beneficiary deed must be recorded with the county recorder during the owner's lifetime to be effective. The beneficiary does not need to sign or even know about the deed. It takes effect only upon the owner's death.

questions.question_text: The primary advantage of a beneficiary deed over a will for transferring real property is:
questions.correct_answer: It avoids the probate process entirely
distractors: It provides better creditor protection
It transfers the property immediately upon signing
It eliminates the need for title insurance
questions.explanation: The main advantage is probate avoidance. The property transfers directly to the beneficiary upon the owner's death without court proceedings. Unlike a will, which must go through probate, the beneficiary deed works automatically.

questions.question_text: A homeowner with a beneficiary deed sells the property to a third party. What happens to the beneficiary deed?
questions.correct_answer: It becomes void since the owner no longer holds title
distractors: The beneficiary deed transfers to the new property owner
The named beneficiary can legally block the sale
The deed remains valid and enforceable against the buyer
questions.explanation: If the owner sells the property, the beneficiary deed becomes moot because the owner no longer holds title to transfer at death. The beneficiary has no interest in the property until the owner's death, and the sale eliminates that possibility.
